content.text: {"design_type":"encouragement","unit_of_randomization_canonical":"individual participant","is_clustered":false,"analysis_unit_canonical":null,"primary_outcomes_dedup":["health care use","health","financial strain"],"arms":[{"arm_id":"arm1","name":"Lottery selected (won opportunity to apply)","role":"treatment","description":"Individuals selected in the lottery (“lottery winners”) who won the opportunity—for themselves and any household member—to apply for Oregon Health Plan Standard (Medicaid).","evidence_quote_ids":["eq1","eq2"]},{"arm_id":"arm2","name":"Not selected in lottery (control group)","role":"control","description":"Individuals not selected in the lottery (controls), who did not receive the opportunity to apply via the lottery selection.","evidence_quote_ids":["eq1"]}],"factors":[],"assignment_rules":["Names were drawn from a Medicaid waiting list by lottery; selected persons won the opportunity to apply for Oregon Health Plan Standard and were sent an application; non-selected persons formed the control group.","Enrollment in Oregon Health Plan Standard occurred only among selected persons who returned the application and met eligibility requirements."],"design_completeness":"partial","extraction_sources":["registry"],"evidence_quotes":[{"id":"eq1","source_doc":"registry","quote":"Treatment group: 29,834 people and 24,912 households selected in the lottery (the lottery winners)\nControl group: 45,088 and 41,473 households not selected (the control group).","supports":"Defines treatment vs control arms as lottery-selected vs not selected, and labels the control group explicitly."},{"id":"eq2","source_doc":"registry","quote":"The state drew names from this waiting list by lottery to fill the openings. Persons who were selected won the opportunity — for themselves and any household member — to apply for Oregon Health Plan Standard and were sent an application. Those who sent back their application and met eligibility requirements were enrolled in Oregon Health Plan Standard.","supports":"Describes the randomized assignment mechanism (lottery) and that selection is an encouragement/eligibility opportunity rather than guaranteed enrollment."}],"notes":"Classified as an encouragement design because randomization determines the opportunity to apply/enroll (selection in lottery), not direct assignment to Medicaid coverage; take-up depends on returning the application and meeting eligibility. Unit of randomization is treated as individual participant (names drawn), though the registry also reports household counts; analysis unit not explicitly stated."}

{"design_type":"other","unit_of_randomization_canonical":null,"is_clustered":false,"analysis_unit_canonical":null,"primary_outcomes_dedup":["Whether an employer calls or emails in response to a submitted resume."],"arms":[],"factors":[],"assignment_rules":[],"design_completeness":"unclear","extraction_sources":["registry"],"evidence_quotes":[{"id":"eq1","source_doc":"registry","quote":"We construct fictitious resumes, randomly vary the institution from which the job applicant received a degree or certificate, and apply to job vacancies that are posted on a large, national job search website.","supports":"Indicates randomized variation in the credentialing institution on resumes; implies an audit/field experiment but does not enumerate arms/cells or unit of randomization explicitly."},{"id":"eq2","source_doc":"registry","quote":"While our primary research question concerns employers’ valuations of a for-profit versus public credential, we also test the impact of having any credential for job vacancies that do not require it.","supports":"Suggests at least two dimensions of treatment variation (for-profit vs public; any credential vs no credential for jobs not requiring it) but does not specify treatment cells/arms."},{"id":"eq3","source_doc":"registry","quote":"EXPERIMENTAL_DESIGN:\nSee the paper and pre-analysis plan for details.","supports":"Registry does not provide experimental design details needed to fully specify arms/factors, assignment rules, or unit of randomization."},{"id":"eq4","source_doc":"registry","quote":"Whether an employer calls or emails in response to a submitted resume.","supports":"Defines the primary outcome (employer callback via call/email)."}],"notes":"Registry text indicates a resume audit field experiment with randomized variation in the institution on fictitious resumes and potentially variation in whether a credential is present for jobs not requiring one. However, the registry does not specify the randomization unit (resume, vacancy/job posting, employer, labor market, etc.), the full set of treatment arms/cells, or assignment rules; it explicitly defers to the paper/pre-analysis plan. Therefore design type is coded as 'other' and key fields are left null/empty."}

{
  "design_type": "simple_multiarm",
  "unit_of_randomization_canonical": "individual participant",
  "is_clustered": false,
  "analysis_unit_canonical": null,
  "primary_outcomes_dedup": [
    "Any hospital readmission",
    "Any emergency department use",
    "Any hospital use (inpatient or ED)",
    "Inpatient readmission from the ED",
    "Inpatient readmission not from the ED",
    "Number of readmissions",
    "Had 2+ readmissions",
    "Number of days in the hospital",
    "Hospital charges",
    "Hospital receipts"
  ],
  "arms": [
    {
      "arm_id": "arm1",
      "name": "Link2Care",
      "role": "treatment",
      "description": "Intensive care management and coordination for up to 6 months following hospital discharge, delivered by a multidisciplinary care team and involving bedside engagement during admission plus home visits and care/social-service navigation support after discharge.",
      "evidence_quote_ids": [
        "eq1"
      ]
    },
    {
      "arm_id": "arm2",
      "name": "Standard Care",
      "role": "control",
      "description": "Standard discharge planning from the hospital with no follow-up by Link2Care team members.",
      "evidence_quote_ids": [
        "eq2"
      ]
    }
  ],
  "factors": [],
  "assignment_rules": [
    "Eligible patients admitted to one of two Camden, New Jersey hospitals (Cooper University Hospital or Our Lady of Lourdes) are recruited prior to discharge and enrolled into the study, which includes randomization to Link2Care versus standard care."
  ],
  "design_completeness": "partial",
  "extraction_sources": [
    "registry"
  ],
  "evidence_quotes": [
    {
      "id": "eq1",
      "source_doc": "registry",
      "quote": "Experimental: Link2Care\nLink2Care provides intensive care management and coordination for up to 6 months following hospital discharge. Participants are assigned to a multidisciplinary care team (Link2Care) comprised of a registered nurse, licensed practical nurse, social worker, intervention specialist, community health worker, and health coaches.",
      "supports": "Defines the Link2Care treatment arm and its content/duration."
    },
    {
      "id": "eq2",
      "source_doc": "registry",
      "quote": "No Intervention: Standard Care\nIndividuals in the standard care arm receive standard discharge planning from the hospital with no followup by Link2Care team members.",
      "supports": "Defines the standard-care control arm."
    },
    {
      "id": "eq3",
      "source_doc": "registry",
      "quote": "A CCHP recruiter will approach individuals deemed eligible before the candidate is discharged from the hospital. The recruiter will explain the purpose, process, risks and benefits of the study, including the randomization process and intervention.",
      "supports": "Indicates randomization occurs at the individual patient level (implied by recruitment of individuals and discussion of randomization)."
    },
    {
      "id": "eq4",
      "source_doc": "registry",
      "quote": "Patients will be enrolled at the two major Camden hospitals: Cooper University Hospital and Our Lady of Lourdes.",
      "supports": "Identifies recruitment sites; supports that the trial enrolls patients (individuals) within hospitals rather than randomizing hospitals."
    }
  ],
  "notes": "Randomization is mentioned but the registry text does not state the precise allocation mechanism (e.g., 1:1 ratio) or whether randomization is stratified (e.g., by hospital). Therefore assignment details are incomplete, but the two-arm individual-level RCT structure is clear."
}

{"design_type":"other","unit_of_randomization_canonical":"individual participant","is_clustered":false,"analysis_unit_canonical":null,"primary_outcomes_dedup":["Degree of favor for alternative regulatory regimes for morally controversial transactions (e.g., procurement and allocation of organs for transplantation)","Choice between alternative regulatory regimes for morally controversial transactions (e.g., procurement and allocation of organs for transplantation)","Degree of moral concern associated with alternative regulatory regimes (e.g., concern for exploitation of the poor and vulnerable, coercion, respect for human dignity)"],"arms":[{"arm_id":"A","name":"System A (unpaid donors; agency allocation)","role":"experimental","description":"Unpaid kidney donors; donors can donate directed or anonymously; kidneys allocated by government-coordinated agency according to priority rules.","evidence_quote_ids":["eq2"]},{"arm_id":"B","name":"System B (public agency pays $20,000; agency allocation)","role":"experimental","description":"Kidney donors can receive $20,000 from a public agency; kidneys allocated by government-coordinated agency; donors may opt out of payment and make directed unpaid donations.","evidence_quote_ids":["eq2"]},{"arm_id":"C","name":"System C (patient pays $20,000; transactions directly/private; agency registry)","role":"experimental","description":"Kidney donors can receive $20,000 directly from recipient; government-coordinated agency keeps registry; transactions occur directly or via private agency; donors may opt out of payment and make directed unpaid donations.","evidence_quote_ids":["eq2"]},{"arm_id":"info_savings","name":"Efficiency info includes taxpayer savings/benefits","role":"experimental","description":"Participant additionally receives information on corresponding financial savings/benefits for taxpayers from additional transplants (in addition to kidney-supply info).","evidence_quote_ids":["eq3"]},{"arm_id":"info_no_savings","name":"Efficiency info excludes taxpayer savings/benefits","role":"experimental","description":"Participant receives only information about number of kidneys procured and share of annual demand covered by each system (no monetary savings).","evidence_quote_ids":["eq3"]},{"arm_id":"study2_cell_1","name":"Study 2: one of eight systems (payment type × amount × payer)","role":"experimental","description":"Study 2 randomly assigns participants to one of eight systems defined by (cash vs non-cash) × ($30K vs $100K) × (government vs patient payer). Specific cell labels not provided in registry text.","evidence_quote_ids":["eq5"]},{"arm_id":"study2_cell_2","name":"Study 2: moral-features elicitation randomized within system (express vs not)","role":"experimental","description":"Within each of the eight Study 2 systems, participants are further randomized (equal probability) to express or not express opinions about some moral features.","evidence_quote_ids":["eq5"]}],"factors":[],"assignment_rules":["Respondents are presented with alternative systems, with random variation in whether compensation is allowed, allocation mechanism (market vs government agency), and estimated gains for society.","In Full-scale Study 1, participants evaluate Systems A/B/C; in choice sets, the expected number of kidneys procured (Y) is randomly assigned across systems, participants, and choice set; and with 50% chance participants also receive taxpayer savings/benefits information.","In Full-scale Study 2, participants are randomly assigned to one of eight systems defined by payment nature, payment amount, and identity of payer; within each system they are further randomized to express (or not) opinions on some moral features; Study 2 also includes a within-subject component of multiple Yes/No choices across kidney supply outcomes."],"design_completeness":"partial","extraction_sources":["registry"],"evidence_quotes":[{"id":"eq1","source_doc":"registry","quote":"For a series of morally controversial transactions, we will present respondents with a set of alternative systems, randomly varying (a) whether some form of compensation to the \"sellers\" is allowed ... (b) whether allocation ... is based on market forces or decided by a government agency, and (c) the (estimated) gains for society associated with the various systems.","supports":"High-level randomized variation dimensions (compensation allowed; allocation mechanism; gains/efficiency)."},{"id":"eq2","source_doc":"registry","quote":"The second screen describes three different systems of procurement and allocation of kidneys from live donors: In System A, unpaid donors... allocated by an agency... In System B, kidney donors can receive $20,000 from a public agency... allocated by an agency... In System C, kidney donors can receive $20,000 directly from the kidney recipient... transactions occur directly... A registry ... is kept by an agency...","supports":"Study 1 explicit systems A/B/C descriptions."},{"id":"eq3","source_doc":"registry","quote":"The value of Y is randomly assigned between systems, participants and choice set... Moreover, with 50% chance, we randomly assign the participant to receive... also the corresponding financial savings or benefits for taxpayers... We include this arm to the experiment...","supports":"Study 1 randomization of efficiency levels and additional info (taxpayer savings) as a separate randomized arm."},{"id":"eq4","source_doc":"registry","quote":"We will then gauge the respondents' degree of moral concern and overall favor for the various systems. Then, we will obtain estimates, at the individual level, of marginal rates of substitution between \"morality\" and \"efficiency\".","supports":"Outcomes measured from respondent ratings/choices; individual-level MRS objective."},{"id":"eq5","source_doc":"registry","quote":"We randomly assigned participants to one of eight alternative organ procurement and allocation systems, where each system was a combination of (a) nature pf payment (cash or non-cash), (b) amount of payment ($30K or $100K), and (c) the identity of payer (government agency or the patient), Within each of the eight systems, we further randomized participants, with equal probability, to also express (or not) opinions about some moral features of the system. We added a within-subject component: each participant made multiple Yes-No choices on their assigned system for different possible kidney supply outcomes.","supports":"Study 2 factorial structure (8 systems) plus additional randomization and within-subject component."},{"id":"eq6","source_doc":"registry","quote":"The key outcome variables are:, (1) Degree of favor for alternative regulatory regimes... (2) Choice between alternative regulatory regimes... (3) Degree of moral concern associated with alternative regulatory regimes...","supports":"Primary outcomes list."}],"notes":"Labeled as an RCT in the registry, but assignment structure mixes (i) between-subject randomizations (e.g., Study 2 2×2×2; Study 1 50% info arm; randomized kidney-supply values) and (ii) substantial within-subject components (ratings of multiple systems; multiple Yes/No choices in Study 2). Because the registry describes two full-scale studies with different randomization schemas and does not provide explicit names for all Study 2 cells, design is marked partial and design_type set to 'other' (mixed/complex)."}

{"design_type":"simple_multiarm","unit_of_randomization_canonical":"individual participant","is_clustered":false,"analysis_unit_canonical":null,"primary_outcomes_dedup":["Post-experiment employment","Post-experiment earnings","Post-experiment reservation wages"],"arms":[{"arm_id":"A1","name":"Coarse evaluation treatment (hired; traditional/coarse evaluation)","role":"treatment","description":"Workers were hired for a 10-hour data-entry job and received an evaluation/comment designed to be equivalent to a typical employer evaluation in the oDesk marketplace (coarse/uninformative feedback, subject to rating thresholds).","evidence_quote_ids":["eq1","eq2","eq3","eq4"]},{"arm_id":"A2","name":"Detailed evaluation treatment (hired; more detailed evaluation)","role":"treatment","description":"Workers were hired for a 10-hour data-entry job and received a more detailed evaluation/comment providing additional objective information about performance (with detailed vs uninformative comment depending on rating thresholds).","evidence_quote_ids":["eq1","eq2","eq3","eq4"]},{"arm_id":"A3","name":"Control (not hired; no evaluation)","role":"control","description":"Workers were not hired and therefore did not receive any evaluation.","evidence_quote_ids":["eq1","eq2"]}],"factors":[],"assignment_rules":["Applicants (workers) were randomly assigned to three groups: control (not hired) or one of two treatment groups (hired with coarse evaluation; hired with detailed evaluation).","Randomization into any treatment group (vs control) was stratified by prior oDesk experience; inexperienced workers had higher probability of being assigned to any treatment than experienced workers (32% vs 15%).","Conditional on being assigned to any treatment, assignment to detailed vs coarse evaluation was 50/50."],"design_completeness":"complete","extraction_sources":["registry"],"evidence_quotes":[{"id":"eq1","source_doc":"registry","quote":"\"The workers were randomly assigned to three groups: (1) a treatment group which was hired and given an evaluation like those traditionally given in the marketplace, (2) a treatment group which was hired and given a more detailed evaluation, and (3) a control group which was not hired and thus did not receive any evaluation.\"","supports":"Existence of 3 arms; identifies control vs two treatment groups and their content (hired + coarse eval; hired + detailed eval; control not hired/no eval)."},{"id":"eq2","source_doc":"registry","quote":"\"Workers were first randomized into either the control group or one of two treatment groups: the detailed evaluation or the coarse evaluation treatment group.\"","supports":"Confirms initial assignment structure: control vs either of two treatment groups."},{"id":"eq3","source_doc":"registry","quote":"\"Randomization into any treatment group was stratified on prior oDesk experience, such that workers without oDesk experience had a higher chance of being in any treatment group (32 percent) than experienced workers (15 percent). Conditional on receiving any treatment, all workers had a 50 percent chance of receiving the detailed evaluation treatment.\"","supports":"Assignment rules: stratification by experience for treatment-vs-control; 50/50 split into detailed vs coarse conditional on treatment."},{"id":"eq4","source_doc":"registry","quote":"\"The coarse evaluation treatment was designed to be equivalent to being hired (and, thus, evaluated) by a typical employer in the marketplace. The detailed evaluation treatment was identical to the coarse evaluation treatment except that it provided the market with more information about some workers’ job performance.\"","supports":"Clarifies what differentiates the two treatment arms (coarse vs detailed evaluation content)."},{"id":"eq5","source_doc":"registry","quote":"\"Post-experiment employment, earnings, and reservation wages of workers in all three experimental groups\"","supports":"Primary outcomes."}],"notes":"This is a three-arm individual-level RCT with stratified randomization into any treatment vs control by prior oDesk experience, and a 50/50 split into detailed vs coarse evaluation conditional on treatment. Registry text notes some hired workers did not return/log hours and thus were not rated; treatments are described as intent-to-hire, but assignment arms are still defined by randomized offer/hire + evaluation type."}
